elements.sent-2411-001: Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown impressive proficiency across a range of natural language processing tasks yet remain vulnerable to adversarial prompts, known as jailbreak attacks, carefully designed to elicit harmful responses from LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-002: Traditional methods rely on manual heuristics, which suffer from limited generalizability.
elements.sent-2411-003: While being automatic, optimization-based attacks often produce unnatural jailbreak prompts that are easy to detect by safety filters or require high computational overhead due to discrete token optimization.
elements.sent-2411-004: Witnessing the limitations of existing jailbreak methods, we introduce Generative Adversarial Suffix Prompter (\proj), a novel framework that combines human-readable prompt generation with Latent Bayesian Optimization (LBO) to improve adversarial suffix creation in a fully black-box setting.
elements.sent-2411-005: Our experiments show that \proj can generate natural jailbreak prompts, significantly improving attack success rates, reducing training times, and accelerating inference speed, thus making it an efficient and scalable solution for red-teaming LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-006: LLMs represent a groundbreaking advancement in technology with a remarkable capacity to master a diverse set of skills, revolutionizing many areas ranging from natural language processing to code and content generation~\cite{Kojima2022, Yao2023, zhao2023survey, romera2024mathematical .}
elements.sent-2411-007: However, the versatility and strong generative capability of LLMs are accompanied by a critical challenge: the potential for producing toxic content~\cite{bommasani2021opportunities,goldstein2023generative .}
elements.sent-2411-008: It has been repeatedly reported that LLMs may inadvertently assimilate and perpetuate harmful biases, stereotypes and hate speech, which are often embedded in the extensive corpora of text used to train these models, raising serious ethical and societal concerns~\cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2311-18580, DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2309-07601, Liu2024790 .}
elements.sent-2411-009: The proliferation of such toxic content can facilitate the spread of misinformation, exacerbate polarization, and contribute to harassment, thereby undermining the beneficial impact of LLMs~\cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2402-15302, DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2309-13788, Wen20231322 .}
elements.sent-2411-010: To reduce the likelihood of generating harmful content, researchers and developers have investigated various strategies to improve the safety alignment of LLMs with predefined values~\cite{ouyang2022training, wang2023aligning, achiam2023gpt .}
elements.sent-2411-011: These strategies involve red teaming~\cite{ganguli2022red and fine-tuning LLMs on carefully curated datasets that emphasize positive values, ethical principles, and factual accuracy~\cite{bai2022training, DBLP:conf/nips/JiLDPZB0SW023, DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2405-17374 .}}
elements.sent-2411-012: While these methods can enhance the overall trustworthiness of LLM outputs, malicious actors can easily craft adversarial prompts to elicit harmful responses.
elements.sent-2411-013: These specially crafted prompts are known as jailbreak attacks~\cite{wei2023jailbrokendoesllmsafety,shayegani2023survey , which exploit model sensitivity to input perturbations to trick LLMs into generating toxic content despite employing safety-alignment mechanisms.}

elements.sent-2411-014: To better characterize the vulnerabilities of LLMs, existing research studies have proposed different methods to craft jailbreak prompts, which primarily fall into heuristic and optimization-based categories.
elements.sent-2411-015: Starting from \cite{wei2023jailbrokendoesllmsafety,shen2024donowcharacterizingevaluating, jailbreakchat , heuristic methods design hand-crafted prompts to bypass the model safety guardrails based on intuitive techniques, such as role-playing~\cite{jin2024guard , low-resource language rewriting~\cite{yong2023low , encryption~\cite{yuan2023gpt , in-context demonstration~\cite{wei2023jailbreak , prompt manipulation and injection~\cite{greshake2023youvesignedforcompromising, perez2022ignorepreviouspromptattack, liu2024formalizingbenchmarkingpromptinjection, melamed2024promptseviltwins .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-016: Despite being highly flexible, these methods require significant manual efforts for prompt engineering and tend to be inconsistent across different inputs and models, thus limited in applicability.
elements.sent-2411-017: In contrast, optimization-based methods \cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2404-16873, chen2024agentpoisonredteamingllmagents employ automatic search techniques by leveraging model gradients or logits to uncover prompts that are likely to elicit harmful LLM responses, offering a more robust and systematic solution.}
elements.sent-2411-018: Nevertheless, these optimized prompts are often different from natural human language, making them easy to identify by perplexity-based filtering strategies.
elements.sent-2411-019: In addition, many of these methods require complex discrete optimization~\cite{Deng_2024, jones2023automaticallyauditinglargelanguage, xu2023llmfoolitselfpromptbased, DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2307-15043, zhao2024acceleratinggreedycoordinategradient, li2024fastergcgefficientdiscreteoptimization, liao2024amplegcglearninguniversaltransferable over the combinatorial token space to produce a single adversarial prompt, incurring substantial computational overhead.}
elements.sent-2411-020: Moreover, hybrid automated attacks~\cite{DBLP:conf/iclr/LiuXCX24, chao2024jailbreakingblackboxlarge, liu2024autodanturbolifelongagentstrategy have been developed that combine optimization techniques with manual jailbreak strategies.}

elements.sent-2411-022: In this paper, we introduce \proj, a novel Generative Adversarial Suffix Prompter that can efficiently generate adversarial suffixes to evade LLM safeguards while preserving human readability in fully black-box environments.
elements.sent-2411-023: Table \ref{table:high-level comparison GASP highlights the key features of \proj, with comparisons to state-of-the-art optimization-based methods, which are detailed below.}
elements.sent-2411-024: This readability makes the prompts harder to detect with perplexity-based filters \cite{DBLP:conf/iclr/0002HTZF24 and enhances their realism, supporting effective red-teaming by simulating real-world adversarial inputs.}
elements.sent-2411-025: The acquisition function within LBO ensures a balance between exploration and exploitation, allowing efficient identification of adversarial prompts.
elements.sent-2411-026: By leveraging feedback from GASPEval in a continuous loop, \proj iteratively fine-tunes suffixes to maximize their bypass potential.
elements.sent-2411-027: In addition, \proj employs Odds-Ratio Preference Optimization (ORPO) \cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2403-07691 to adjust the model parameters, amplifying the effectiveness of high-success suffixes while maintaining readability and coherence.}
elements.sent-2411-028: Such a dual feedback mechanism, informed by real-time model responses, enhances \proj's attack efficacy against evolving LLM safeguard mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-029: By uncovering vulnerabilities with minimal access, \proj supports robust red-teaming, identifying potential weaknesses in deployed LLMs and contributing to improved model security.
elements.sent-2411-030: In this work, we focus on generating adversarial suffixes for jailbreaking LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-031: This section introduces formal mathematical notations and foundational concepts to help readers better understand suffix-based jailbreak attacks.
elements.sent-2411-032: Specifically, let \( f_\theta: \mathcal{X \rightarrow \mathcal{Y \) be a given black-box LLM, denoted as TargetLLM throughout the paper, that the adversary aims to jailbreak.}}
elements.sent-2411-033: Here, $\mathcal{X}$ is the input space of textual prompts, $\mathcal{Y}$ is the output space of LLM responses, and \( \theta \) denotes the model parameters potentially involved by some safeguard fine-tuning techniques to align the model behavior with predefined values.
elements.sent-2411-034: Let $x\in\mathcal{X _{\mathrm{harm }$ like ``how to build a bomb,'' where $\mathcal{X _{\mathrm{harm }$ is a collection of harmful prompts or those designed to test the safety mechanisms of $f_\theta$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-035: Due to the safety alignment, the model response $f_\theta(x)$ typically includes refusals, such as ``Sorry, I cannot assist with that request,'' to react to the harmful content or unethical intent embedded in the query prompt.
elements.sent-2411-036: This ensures the model satisfies the safety rules even when prompted by harmful prompts.
elements.sent-2411-037: The goal of suffix-based jailbreak attacks is to search for an optimal sequence of tokens $e$, denoted as an adversarial suffix~\cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2307-15043 , such that when attached to the original input $x$, the response elicited from TargetLLM $f_{\theta (x+e)$ will become harmful.}}
elements.sent-2411-038: Let $y\in\mathcal{Y}$ be the desirable affirmative response (e.g., ``Sure, here is how to build a bomb...'') that the attacker aims to elicit from TargetLLM $f_\theta$ corresponding to the original harmful prompt $x\in\mathcal{X _{\mathrm{harm }$, then the adversarial objective is defined as the following optimization problem: \begin{equation}\label{eq:adv obj} \max_{e \in E} \ L_\theta(x, e),  where  L_\theta(x, e) := p_{\theta}(y \mid x + e).\end{equation}.}}
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}\label{eq:adv obj} \max_{e \in E} \ L_\theta(x, e),  where  L_\theta(x, e) := p_{\theta}(y \mid x + e).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-039: Here, \( \mathcal{E \) stands for the feasible set of sequences of tokens that can be appended to the input $x$, and \( p_{\theta (y \mid x + e) \) denotes the adversarial loss function that measures the success of the attack, i.e., how likely the desirable harmful response $y$ can be generated under the $f_\theta$'s output distribution with respect to the suffix-appended input $x+e$.}}
elements.sent-2411-040: To be more specific, we aim to ensure that the suffix \( e \) blends into the original input \( x \) and that the entire prompt \( x + e \) appears natural and coherent.
elements.sent-2411-041: This is important because a prompt that is too unnatural or obfuscated may be flagged by either human moderators or automated defenses~\cite{DBLP:conf/iclr/0002HTZF24 .}
elements.sent-2411-042: To enforce this, we define a readability constraint based on the probability that the adversarial prompt \( x + e \) falls within the natural language distribution \( p_{\mathrm{nat \).}}
elements.sent-2411-043: Formally, we define the feasible set for the suffix $\mathcal{E}$ as: \begin{equation}\label{eq:readability} E = \{e: \ R(e) \geq \lambda \} ,  where  R(e) := p_{nat}(x + e).\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}\label{eq:readability} E = \{e: \ R(e) \geq \lambda \} ,  where  R(e) := p_{nat}(x + e).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-044: Here, \( p_{\mathrm{nat (x + e) \) denotes the probability that the adversarial prompt \( x+e \) is perceived as part of the natural language distribution, and \( \phi \) is a hyperparameter that sets the threshold for readability.}}
elements.sent-2411-045: If \( p_{\mathrm{nat (x + e) \geq \lambda \), then the adversarial prompt is considered sufficiently readable, where a higher \( \lambda \) imposes a stricter readability requirement.}}
elements.sent-2411-046: With both the objective for generating adversarial suffix and human-readability constraints in place, suffix-based jailbreak attacks can be thus formulated as a constrained optimization problem with respect to TargetLLM $f_\theta$, original input prompt $x$, desired harmful response $y$ and natural language distribution $p_{\mathrm{nat }$ as follows: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:1} \max_{e} \ p_{\theta}(y \mid x + e),  s.t.  \ p_{nat}(x + e) \geq \lambda.\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:1} \max_{e} \ p_{\theta}(y \mid x + e),  s.t.  \ p_{nat}(x + e) \geq \lambda.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-047: Equation \ref{eqn:1 provides a generic optimization framework for adversarial suffix generation, largely guiding our design of \proj.}
elements.sent-2411-048: By minimizing the adversarial loss while incorporating human readability as a constraint, the framework ensures that the optimized prompts remain effective in jailbreaking TargetLLM and sufficiently natural to be indistinguishable from legitimate inputs by human evaluations.
elements.sent-2411-049: We note that prior literature has explored other types of adversarial transformations, especially for heuristic methods that manually craft jailbreak prompts~\cite{wei2023jailbrokendoesllmsafety .}
elements.sent-2411-050: Our focus on adversarial suffix injection aligns with the existing line of research on optimization-based attacks \cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2307-15043, DBLP:conf/iclr/LiuXCX24, DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2404-16873 , primarily due to the autoregressive nature of language models and the ease of optimization.}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=0.9\linewidth]{Figures/framework.pdf} \caption{Summary of the proposed \proj framework: A) Pre-training of SuffixLLM with the use of AdvSuffixes, enabling it to create generic suffixes that serve as a base for adaptable jailbreak prompts; B) Alignment of SuffixLLM using LBO to establish a distribution aligned closely with the TargetLLM, enhancing its ability to produce effective jailbreak prompts; C) An adaptive fine-tuning mechanism is implemented to dynamically refine and adjust suffixes in real-time by querying the TargetLLM, ensuring optimal performance for each generated prompt; D) The produced SuffixLLM's output distribution is expected to be similar to that of the TargetLLM.} \label{fig:summary}
elements.sent-2411-051: In this section, we elaborate on the design insights and key modules of the proposed framework \proj, including the goal of SuffixLLM (Section \ref{sec:SuffixLLM ), the role of AdvSuffixes (Section \ref{sec:AdvSuffixes ), and how LBO and OPRO are employed to solve the above-mentioned challenges (Section \ref{sec:LBO and OPRO ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-052: The working mechanism of GASP is illustrated in Figure \ref{fig:summary .}
elements.sent-2411-053: To generate suffixes that align with the targeted adversarial objective (Equation \ref{eq:adv obj ) and satisfy the human readability constraint (Equation \ref{eq:readability ), we propose to create a specialized LLM, referred to as SuffixLLM, $g_\phi:\mathcal{X}\rightarrow \mathcal{E}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-054: This model can be specifically trained to generate coherent adversarial suffixes $g_\phi(x)$ across various input prompts $x\in\mathcal{X _{\mathrm{harm }$ for a designated TargetLLM $f_\theta$.}}
elements.sent-2411-055: To effectively engage with TargetLLM, the objective of SuffixLLM centers on approximating the conditional probability \( p_\theta(y \mid x + e) \), which reflects the likelihood of TargetLLM generating desirable harmful responses \( y\in\mathcal{Y \) when presented with a crafted suffix \( e \) appended to the prompt \( x \).}
elements.sent-2411-056: The primary goal is to exploit the specific vulnerabilities of TargetLLM, allowing it to generate suffixes that increase the chances of eliciting harmful responses that align closely with the target model's inherent behavior.
elements.sent-2411-057: By learning to replicate the response distribution of TargetLLM, SuffixLLM operates under a multi-objective optimization framework (Equation~\ref{eqn:1 ) that emphasizes both the effectiveness of adversarial prompts and their coherence in human language.}
elements.sent-2411-058: This involves understanding the specific output tendencies of the target model, allowing SuffixLLM to craft suffixes that elicit harmful responses in ways that appear indistinguishable from typical conversations.
elements.sent-2411-059: Ultimately, the successful alignment of SuffixLLM's generated suffixes with TargetLLM's harmful response distribution not only increases the effectiveness of the adversarial strategy but also preserves the naturalness required for human evaluators.
elements.sent-2411-060: These features enable our method to generate adversarial suffixes with high jailbreak success for any input prompt $x$ and TargetLLM using only black-box model information (i.e., raw outputs).
elements.sent-2411-061: In contrast, most existing optimization-based jailbreak methods~\cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2307-15043, DBLP:conf/iclr/LiuXCX24 are white-box attacks, which first create universal adversarial suffixes by leveraging the gradient information with white-box surrogate LLMs then transfer to TargetLLM.}
elements.sent-2411-062: An exception is AdvPrompter~\cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2404-16873 that uses log probability outputs of the target LLM for suffix optimization, but still is gray-box.}
elements.sent-2411-063: Establishing a baseline distribution is essential for guiding effective prompt generation, as it provides a reference point that captures a generic probability distribution of harmful responses across various adversarial suffixes.
elements.sent-2411-064: By training SuffixLLM on a baseline distribution, we aim to initially approximate the conditions under which an LLM might produce harmful outputs, without yet targeting the specific vulnerabilities of a particular model.
elements.sent-2411-065: This initial phase relies on AdvSuffixes, a curated dataset of adversarial suffixes for diverse prompts, designed to capture typical patterns in harmful responses.
elements.sent-2411-066: Using a two-shot prompting technique on an uncensored model, we generated adversarial suffixes aligned with well-known jailbreak styles, which are proven to achieve high attack success rates across models.
elements.sent-2411-067: Inspired by AdvBench \cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2307-15043 , this approach enabled us to create a dataset of suffixes that establishes a baseline distribution of adversarial prompts.}
elements.sent-2411-068: More details on the creation of AdvSuffixes are provided in Appendix~\ref{sec:advsuffixes .}
elements.sent-2411-069: Leveraging AdvSuffixes allows us to approximate a generic adversarial distribution \( p_{\mathrm{gen (y \mid x+e) \), where \( y \) represents possible harmful outputs with respect to the input prompt $x$, aiming to capture a broad range of adversarial scenarios and lay the foundation of our \proj framework (Module A in Figure \ref{fig:summary ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-070: Formally, the objective is to develop a dataset of adversarial prompts and suffixes that, when used with specific LLMs, produces a response distribution approximating a general harmful response pattern, aligning the conditional response distribution of these LLMs with an overarching, representative pattern of harmful outputs: \begin{equation}p(y \mid x+e, \ AdvSuffixes) \approx p_gen(y \mid x+e),\end{equation} where \( p(y \mid x+e, \ \mathrm{AdvSuffixes ) \) serves as an approximation of harmful response tendencies when suffixes \( e \) from AdvSuffixes are appended to original prompts \( x \).}
elements.sent-2411-071: This distribution provides a generalized adversarial baseline to pre-train SuffixLLM, which can later be fine-tuned to match a specific target model’s unique response characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-072: The baseline distribution generated by SuffixLLM after pre-training on AdvSuffixes might be too generic to be effective in jailbreaking a particular TargetLLM.
elements.sent-2411-073: Therefore, we propose to fine-tune SuffixLLM to align the generic adversarial distribution \( p_{\mathrm{gen (y \mid x+e) \) with the desirable harmful response distribution \( p_\theta(y \mid x+e) \), specifically for TargetLLM.}}
elements.sent-2411-074: Nevertheless, a fundamental challenge lies in the difficulty of calculating loss function value when dealing with discrete token spaces.
elements.sent-2411-075: Unlike continuous optimization problems, where gradient-based methods can efficiently find minima, discrete token spaces—like the words and phrases that constitute natural language—are far more difficult to navigate \cite{DBLP:conf/conll/MarciniakS05 .}
elements.sent-2411-076: We address these challenges in our \proj framework by employing LBO for efficient searching within a latent embedding space and dynamic refinement of SuffixLLM with ORPO and a novel evaluator GASPEval (Modules B and C in Figure \ref{fig:summary ).}
elements.sent-2411-077: Note that each token sequence variation~\cite{wang2024functionalhomotopysmoothingdiscrete can lead to abrupt changes in the model’s behavior, making the search inherently discontinuous.}
elements.sent-2411-078: To address this, we transition to the embedding space defined by the SuffixLLM, where token sequences are represented as continuous vectors.
elements.sent-2411-079: Each suffix \( e \) is mapped to a continuous vector \( z_e \in \mathbb{R ^d \), allowing for gradual adjustments in language meaning and structure, which facilitates optimization.}
elements.sent-2411-080: In the following, we will employ LBO to search an optimal suffix embedding vector \cite{tennenholtz2024demystifyingembeddingspacesusing, simhi2023interpretingembeddingspacesconceptualization , since evaluating all token combinations generated by SuffixLLM is computationally infeasible.}
elements.sent-2411-081: By operating in the embedding space, LBO reduces the search complexity, allowing for a more structured exploration of potential suffixes, which in turn facilitates a smoother and more efficient alignment with TargetLLM's response distribution.
elements.sent-2411-082: By focusing on promising candidates, LBO minimizes the search complexity, improving the exploration of suffixes.
elements.sent-2411-083: To enhance the interpretability of the high-dimensional space, we use t-SNE \cite{JMLR:v9:vandermaaten08a to project \( z_e \) into a lower-dimensional representation, preserving key variations in token sequence behavior.}
elements.sent-2411-084: This approach aligns our search with the response distribution of TargetLLM, ensuring a more efficient optimization process.
elements.sent-2411-085: Specifically, the objective of LBO is to identify the optimal suffix embedding \( z_{e ^* \) that maximizes the likelihood of producing a harmful response from the TargetLLM, while satisfying the human readability constraint: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:lbo} z_e^* = \argmax_{z_e \in R^d} \ p_\theta(y \mid x+e),  s.t.  p_{nat}(x + e) \geq \lambda.\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-005: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:lbo} z_e^* = \argmax_{z_e \in R^d} \ p_\theta(y \mid x+e),  s.t.  p_{nat}(x + e) \geq \lambda.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-086: LBO leverages probabilistic exploration \cite{maus2024jointcompositelatentspace across the embedding space, assessing candidate embeddings by evaluating their likelihood to improve adversarial outcomes.}
elements.sent-2411-087: This probabilistic approach relies on posterior predictions from a Gaussian Process (GP) model, which is continuously updated with each embedding evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-088: By refining its posterior with each new data point, the GP model helps LBO identify regions \cite{gardner2014bayesian where embeddings are more likely to prompt harmful responses, effectively guiding the search toward areas of high adversarial potential.}
elements.sent-2411-089: This iterative feedback loop allows LBO to balance the exploration of new regions and exploitation of high-potential areas, facilitating a comprehensive search across the embedding space.
elements.sent-2411-090: As shown in Figure~\ref{fig:lbo_algo , the marginal distributions illustrate the evolving posterior over various regions, guiding the search toward areas more likely to yield effective adversarial suffixes.}
elements.sent-2411-091: This process allows SuffixLLM to learn from the refined posterior, adapting its adversarial suffix generation to better align with TargetLLM’s behavioral patterns.

Data points, scored by GASPEval, reveal a path through regions of high probability, aiding in efficient search through complex probabilistic spaces.} \label{fig:lbo_algo}
elements.sent-2411-092: However, a successful optimization process also requires an accurate evaluation of whether a given suffix elicits a truly harmful response, which brings us to the challenge of response evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-093: To address the challenge of accurately evaluating whether a suffix has successfully induced a harmful response from the TargetLLM, we introduce a novel evaluator GASPEval, which leverages a secondary model called JudgeLLM.
elements.sent-2411-094: This evaluator is designed to assess the TargetLLM's responses with precision, determining not only if the response contains harmful content but also ensuring it adheres to the adversarial intent while maintaining coherence and readability.
elements.sent-2411-095: Our GASPEval utilizes a diverse set of $21$ rigorous evaluation questions specifically crafted to cover a wide spectrum of harmful behaviors, ranging from subtle misinformation to overtly harmful content like profanity, threats, or illegal suggestions.
elements.sent-2411-096: These questions serve as guidelines for evaluating the degree of harm and the degree of human readability (hence, satisfying the conditions seen in Equation~\ref{eqn:lbo ) in each response and are seen to be superior to other evaluators.}
elements.sent-2411-097: Further details on the evaluator and its advantages are provided in Appendix~\ref{sec:gaspeval .}
elements.sent-2411-098: To enhance SuffixLLM, we incorporate targeted fine-tuning based on the LBO-driven search results, further supported by ORPO \cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2403-07691 .}
elements.sent-2411-099: ORPO's use of an odds ratio-based penalty refines the fine-tuning process by aligning the model outputs with specific preferences.
elements.sent-2411-100: Unlike traditional methods focusing solely on maximizing the likelihood of target responses, ORPO introduces a relative ratio loss component that increases the chances of generating adversarial suffixes while ensuring they remain human-readable.
elements.sent-2411-101: This refined fine-tuning process enables SuffixLLM to prioritize the creation of adversarial suffixes that are more likely to bypass safety mechanisms in TargetLLM while maintaining the suffix coherence and closely adhering to the desired adversarial characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-102: We leverage LBO and GASPEval to guide the search within the embedding space, iteratively identifying and refining suffix embeddings \( z_e \).
elements.sent-2411-103: Through LBO, the GP model is aligned with the TargetLLM, providing probabilistic predictions that optimize the likelihood of generating harmful responses.
elements.sent-2411-104: As a result, SuffixLLM dynamically learns from the evolving GP distribution, progressively refining the suffix embeddings \( z_e \) to more accurately approximate \( p_\theta \).
elements.sent-2411-105: This is achieved through the integration of ORPO, which modifies the baseline distribution \( p_\mathrm{gen \) into a refined distribution \( p_\mathrm{align (y \mid x + e) \), then progressively optimized to match the target \( p_\theta(y \mid x + e) \) as closely as possible.}}
elements.sent-2411-106: Through LBO and ORPO, SuffixLLM not only learns to generate adversarial suffixes more effectively but also becomes increasingly capable of operating within the target’s unique response framework, resulting in a finely tuned model that continuously adapts to the target’s specific vulnerabilities.

elements.sent-2411-152: This section reviews the literature on jailbreak attacks from heuristic to automated optimization methods, as well as various prompt optimization strategies that influence LLM outputs.
elements.sent-2411-153: Additionally, we discuss defense mechanisms aimed at safeguarding LLMs from adversarial manipulation.
elements.sent-2411-154: In addition to heuristic approaches that craft adversarial prompts manually~\cite{wei2023jailbrokendoesllmsafety,shen2024donowcharacterizingevaluating, yong2024lowresourcelanguagesjailbreakgpt4, jailbreakchat,jin2024guard,yong2023low,yuan2023gpt,wei2023jailbreak,greshake2023youvesignedforcompromising, perez2022ignorepreviouspromptattack, liu2024formalizingbenchmarkingpromptinjection, melamed2024promptseviltwins , automatic jailbreak methods~\cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2307-15043, DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2404-16873, DBLP:conf/iclr/LiuXCX24, chao2024jailbreakingblackboxlarge, chen2024agentpoisonredteamingllmagents, Deng_2024 have been proposed to enhance search scalability and efficiency by leveraging distinct optimization strategies.}}
elements.sent-2411-155: For example, the GCG algorithm \cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2307-15043, zhao2024acceleratinggreedycoordinategradient, li2024fastergcgefficientdiscreteoptimization, liao2024amplegcglearninguniversaltransferable , inspired by AutoPrompt \cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2010-15980 , utilizes discrete optimization to identify token replacements that maximize adversarial success rates.}}
elements.sent-2411-156: By selecting tokens iteratively, GCG ensures effective prompts, albeit at the cost of limited coherence and inapplicability to models without gradient access.
elements.sent-2411-157: In contrast, AdvPrompter \cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2404-16873 employs a conditional adversarial suffix generator, which efficiently adapts to unseen instructions by training on parameterized data.}
elements.sent-2411-158: While AdvPrompter demonstrates strong performance in grey-box settings, its reliance on high-quality training data can limit adaptability to novel or less-structured inputs \cite{DBLP:journals/nn/KimKH24 .}
elements.sent-2411-159: In addition, hybrid approaches have also emerged, which combine manual jailbreak tactics with automated optimization to enhance flexibility and generalization.
elements.sent-2411-160: AutoDAN \cite{DBLP:conf/iclr/LiuXCX24, liu2024autodanturbolifelongagentstrategy exemplifies this trend, utilizing a hierarchical genetic algorithm to refine prompts through crossover and mutation.}
elements.sent-2411-161: This iterative process generates universal jailbreaks applicable across diverse models, though it is computationally intensive due to its reliance on evolutionary methods.
elements.sent-2411-162: Similarly, PAIR \cite{chao2024jailbreakingblackboxlarge automates semantic jailbreak generation by leveraging an attacker LLM and querying the target LLM.}
elements.sent-2411-163: PAIR refines candidate jailbreaks through repeated alignment, offering a scalable solution for effective adversarial prompts with reduced manual intervention.
elements.sent-2411-164: Our work (\proj) introduces a novel approach by focusing on adversarial suffix creation through a generative adversarial framework, which does not rely on manual prompt crafting or the iterative optimization methods commonly used in previous work.
elements.sent-2411-165: Unlike existing strategies that often require extensive training or gradient access, our approach is designed to operate effectively in fully black-box settings, allowing for greater flexibility and applicability across a wider range of LLMs without the need for detailed model knowledge or high-quality training data.
elements.sent-2411-166: Soft prompt optimization \cite{liu2024jailbreakingchatgptpromptengineering, lester2021powerscaleparameterefficientprompt, li2021prefixtuningoptimizingcontinuousprompts adjusts token embeddings while freezing model parameters, making it a resource-efficient approach for task-specific tuning.}
elements.sent-2411-167: By contrast, hard prompt optimization \cite{jones2023automaticallyauditinglargelanguage, xu2023llmfoolitselfpromptbased directly modifies input text to influence model outputs.}
elements.sent-2411-168: Techniques like AutoPrompt \cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2010-15980 and Black-box Prompt Optimization \cite{cheng2024blackboxpromptoptimizationaligning excel in applications such as text classification and fact retrieval by employing discrete token searches or black-box optimization to adapt prompts.}}
elements.sent-2411-169: These methods demonstrate the versatility of hard prompt optimization, especially for cases where model internals are inaccessible.
elements.sent-2411-170: Our work builds on these advancements by integrating prompt optimization with adversarial generation techniques, focusing specifically on automated prompt refinement that optimizes LLM jailbreaks without compromising human readability.
elements.sent-2411-171: For instance, SmoothLLM \cite{robey2024smoothllmdefendinglargelanguage adopts a scatter-gather approach to address character-level perturbations in adversarial prompts.}
elements.sent-2411-172: By averaging the model's responses across perturbed variations of the input, SmoothLLM diminishes the impact of adversarial content on the final output.
elements.sent-2411-173: NeMo-Guardrails \cite{rebedea2023nemoguardrailstoolkitcontrollable , an open-source framework, focuses on enforcing safety mechanisms through programmable conversational guardrails.}
elements.sent-2411-174: These guardrails constrain model behavior to prevent harmful or unintended outputs.
elements.sent-2411-175: Reinforcement Learning with Human Feedback (RLHF) \cite{bai2022traininghelpfulharmlessassistant, qi2023finetuningalignedlanguagemodels remains one of the most widely adopted safety strategies, training a reward model using adversarial prompts and responses.}
elements.sent-2411-176: By incorporating this reward model into the RLHF pipeline, LLMs are safety-tuned to prioritize ethical and safe outputs during interactions.
elements.sent-2411-177: In the future, we plan to test the adaptability of our attack framework against LLMs that employ the defenses above, regardless of implicit or explicit strategies.
elements.sent-2411-178: In addition, integrating our generative approach of adversarial prompts into the safeguard fine-tuning process could be an interesting future direction and a promising strategy to enhance the resilience of LLMs against jailbreak attacks.

elements.sent-2411-179: To establish a rigorous benchmark for adversarial testing, we create AdvSuffixes, a comprehensive dataset of adversarial suffixes designed to elicit (generic) harmful responses from LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-180: In particular, we generate these suffixes on the Llama-3.1-8B-Lexi-Uncensored-V2 model using a two-shot prompt technique with various common but diverse jailbreak styles for guidance.
elements.sent-2411-181: This approach enabled us to produce adversarial suffixes for each of the $519$ harmful instructions drawn from the AdvBench dataset~\cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2307-15043 .}
elements.sent-2411-182: AdvBench initially provides $127$ prompts that have proven extremely challenging to jailbreak LLMs such as LLaMA-2.
elements.sent-2411-183: These prompts consistently fail to bypass the model’s safeguards, highlighting their high difficulty and resilience against existing adversarial techniques.
elements.sent-2411-184: Given the dependency of AdvPrompter and similar methods on the distribution of training and testing data, these $127$ prompts were ideal for assessing in-distribution performance but insufficient to fully evaluate generalization.
elements.sent-2411-185: To address this limitation, we generated an additional set of $100$ prompts using the uncensored model.
elements.sent-2411-186: The process involved using the original $127$ AdvBench prompts as input, paired with guiding instructions to create prompts of comparable difficulty while ensuring they remained independent of the original training distribution.
elements.sent-2411-187: This approach introduced deliberate variations in syntax and phrasing, producing prompts that deviate from the patterns of the original AdvBench set.
elements.sent-2411-188: By leveraging an uncensored model and tailored instructions, we crafted prompts that retained the high difficulty level of the $127$ prompts while incorporating distinct adversarial structures.
elements.sent-2411-189: This ensures the new dataset possesses out-of-distribution characteristics, challenging frameworks to generalize effectively beyond familiar correlations.
elements.sent-2411-190: Consequently, this expanded dataset provides a more comprehensive benchmark for evaluating both in-distribution performance and generalization capabilities, while rigorously testing model adaptability and the robustness of adversarial defenses.
elements.sent-2411-191: Through iterative generation and human filtering, we amassed over $11763$ adversarial suffixes in total (averaging $22.6$ suffixes per prompt), designed to be appended to prompts with the goal of eliciting harmful responses from LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-192: This diverse collection encompasses a range of malicious content, including profanity, graphic descriptions, threats, and misinformation, providing a rigorous tool-set for testing model vulnerabilities.

Train Suffix Model on Adversarial Suffixes}{} \Train{ SuffixLLM $\leftarrow \text{Train}(SuffixLLM, \mathcal{A})$ \tcp{Train SuffixLLM on adversarial suffixes from $\mathcal{A}$} } \SetKwBlock{Generate}{2. Generate generic suffixes}{} \Generate{ $\mathcal{G} \leftarrow SuffixLLM.\text{generate}(n)$ \tcp{Generate a set $\mathcal{G}$ of $n$ candidate suffixes} } \SetKwBlock{Identify}{3. Identify effective suffixes and finetune with GASPTrain}{} \Identify{ $\mathcal{G}^* \leftarrow \text{GASPTrain}(TargetLLM, \mathcal{G})$ \tcp{Use GASPTrain to select effective suffixes that induce jailbreaking behavior and finetune TargetLLM to create such suffixes, specific to TargetLLM} } \Return{SuffixLLM} \tcp{Aligned SuffixLLM that produces successful adversarial prompts}
elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{GASPTrain} \label{alg:GASPTrain} \KwIn{Target model TargetLLM, original prompt $\mathcal{X}$, candidate suffix set $\mathcal{G}_0$, evaluator GASPEval} \KwOut{Set of optimized adversarial suffixes $\mathcal{G}^*$} \SetKwBlock{Init}{1. Initialize LBO}{} \Init{ Define embedding space $\mathcal{E}$ for suffix candidates Configure LBO to explore $\mathcal{E}$ with acquisition function $a(x)$ \tcp{Balance exploration and exploitation in search for optimal suffixes} } \SetKwBlock{Eval}{\textbf{2. Evaluate and restructure suffixes using GASPEval}}{} \Eval{ \ForEach{$g \in \mathcal{G}_0$}{ $\text{response} \leftarrow TargetLLM([X + g])$ \tcp{Query TargetLLM with prompt $[X + g]$ and record its response} $s(g) \leftarrow GASPEval(\text{response})$ \tcp{Evaluate response via GASPEval by querying JudgeLLM with 21 evaluation questions} } $\mathcal{G}_0 \leftarrow \text{Sort}(\mathcal{G}_0, \text{key} = s(g), \text{descending})$ \tcp{Restructure $\mathcal{G}_0$ by ordering suffixes from most to least effective based on scores} } \SetKwBlock{FineTune}{\textbf{3. Fine-Tune SuffixLLM with Top Suffixes}}{} \FineTune{ SuffixLLM $\leftarrow \text{ORPO}(SuffixLLM, \mathcal{G}_0)$ \tcp{Retrain SuffixLLM on the top-performing suffixes to refine suffix generation via ORPO} } \Return{$\mathcal{G}_0$}

elements.section-2411-011: Detailed Explanations of LBO and ORPO
elements.sent-2411-211: In the context of adversarial prompting, a central challenge arises from the inability to directly access the target model's probability distribution \( p_{\theta (y \mid x + e) \), where \( y \) represents the desired harmful responses given a prompt \( x \) and an appended suffix \( e \).}
elements.sent-2411-212: Since we operate under black-box constraints, obtaining \( p_{\theta (y \mid x + e) \) is infeasible, necessitating an alternative approach to approximate the distribution without direct access to the target model's internals.}
elements.sent-2411-213: Therefore, LBO and ORPO are introduced in the \proj framework to address the aforementioned challenges.
elements.sent-2411-214: To address this challenge, \proj employs LBO within an embedding space.
elements.sent-2411-215: As stated earlier, this strategy involves mapping the suffix \( e \) into a continuous vector representation \( z_e \in \mathbb{R ^d \), transforming the discrete nature of token generation into a smooth manifold that is amenable to optimization.}
elements.sent-2411-216: The evaluation function \( f(x, e; \theta) \) serves as a proxy to the target model’s output, providing a continuous measure of performance based on the generated suffixes.
elements.sent-2411-217: This function assists in approximating an output probability \( p(y \mid x+e) \), which reflects the likelihood of generating harmful responses in TargetLLM.
elements.sent-2411-218: The crux of LBO lies in its acquisition function, which enables systematic exploration of the embedding space.
elements.sent-2411-219: A commonly used acquisition function in this context is the Expected Improvement (EI), defined as: \begin{equation}\mathrm{EI}(z_e) = \mathbb{E} \big[\max(0, f_{\mathrm{best}} - f(z_e)) \big],\end{equation} where \( f_{\mathrm{best \) denotes the best-observed performance from previous evaluations, and \( f(z_e) \) is the predicted output of the Gaussian Process (GP) model at the new suffix embedding \( z_e \).}}
elements.sent-2411-220: The EI function quantifies the expected gain in performance from sampling at \( z_e \), encouraging the selection of suffixes that are likely to yield significant improvements over the current best.
elements.sent-2411-221: Before applying the EI function, the GP model plays a critical role in capturing the underlying distribution of responses generated by the target model.
elements.sent-2411-222: The GP model assumes a prior distribution over functions and is updated with each new evaluation to create a posterior distribution.
elements.sent-2411-223: This posterior reflects the model's uncertainty and mean predictions, which are used in the acquisition function to inform future sampling.
elements.sent-2411-224: Specifically, the GP provides both a mean prediction \( \mu(z_e) \) and an associated uncertainty \( \sigma(z_e) \) for each candidate suffix embedding, enabling LBO to make informed decisions (via EI) about where to sample next.
elements.sent-2411-225: By iteratively updating the GP model with evaluations obtained through \( f \) or GASPEval, the algorithm refines its estimates of \( p_{\theta (y \mid x + e) \).}
elements.sent-2411-226: As new evaluations are added to the dataset, the GP becomes increasingly adept at modeling the TargetLLM's response distribution.

elements.sent-2411-237: We use ORPO in finetuning SuffixLLM due to its innovative approach to aligning model outputs with specific preference criteria, which is critical for adversarial prompt optimization.
elements.sent-2411-238: ORPO introduces a novel odds ratio-based penalty to the traditional negative log-likelihood (NLL) loss, enabling the model to differentiate between favored and disfavored response styles.
elements.sent-2411-239: Unlike standard supervised fine-tuning that focuses solely on maximizing the likelihood of target responses, ORPO's relative ratio loss component maximizes the odds of generating the desired response over undesired ones.
elements.sent-2411-240: This is achieved by increasing the log odds ratio between preferred and rejected responses, effectively discouraging the model from producing outputs that deviate from optimal adversarial characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-241: The penalty component of ORPO not only corrects unwanted tendencies in the model’s predictions but also amplifies gradient updates when the likelihood of undesired outputs is too high, pushing the model more firmly toward the preferred response style.
elements.sent-2411-242: By penalizing wrong predictions and emphasizing contrastive alignment between chosen and rejected responses, ORPO ensures that SuffixLLM is better attuned to produce robust and context-sensitive adversarial prompts.
elements.sent-2411-243: Such a fine-grained control enables SuffixLLM to adapt to specialized jailbreak contexts more efficiently than with standard finetuning methods.

elements.sent-2411-244: GASPEval is a dual-purpose component within our framework, designed to not only steer the LBO process through the complex probability landscape but also to serve as a comprehensive evaluation mechanism for adversarial prompts generated by different jailbreak attack methods.
elements.sent-2411-245: On the one hand, GASPEval plays a pivotal role in guiding LBO by providing structured feedback during the optimization process, enabling it to explore and navigate continuous embedding spaces effectively.
elements.sent-2411-246: This guidance is essential for narrowing down the search space to identify suffixes that maximize attack success rates, thus enhancing the efficiency and effectiveness of adversarial suffix generation.
elements.sent-2411-247: By acting as a structured pathfinder, GASPEval ensures that LBO can consistently generate optimized suffixes in a fully black-box setting (see Figure ~\ref{fig:lbo_algo ).}
elements.sent-2411-248: In addition to its optimization function, GASPEval also serves as a robust evaluation tool, capable of assessing the harmfulness and readability of responses generated by different adversarial frameworks.
elements.sent-2411-249: While StrongREJECT is known for its rigorous grading standards, it often proves overly strict, which can hinder LBO’s alignment by penalizing potential adversarial prompts too harshly.
elements.sent-2411-250: On the other hand, simpler evaluation approaches, such as Keyword Matching, can lead to high rates of false positives (FP) and true negatives (TN), ultimately skewing results.
elements.sent-2411-251: GASPEval achieves a balanced approach by applying a moderate scoring scheme, which is neither excessively harsh nor overly permissive.
elements.sent-2411-252: This balanced evaluation enables consistent guidance for LBO while also providing a more reliable assessment than traditional evaluators, ensuring that the generated prompts are coherent, effective, and more accurately aligned to bypass detection mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-253: We first explain the limitations of existing evaluation methods for jailbreak attacks, namely Keyword Matching and StrongREJECT.
elements.sent-2411-254: In particular, Keyword Matching often misclassifies benign or contextually appropriate responses as harmful by flagging isolated words or phrases that appear on a predefined list.
elements.sent-2411-255: This approach leads to FPs, where non-harmful content is mistakenly labeled as harmful, disrupting natural user interactions.
elements.sent-2411-256: In addition, StrongREJECT, an aggressive filtering method aimed at blocking adversarial inputs, frequently misses subtle yet harmful responses, resulting in TNs that allow genuinely harmful content to evade detection.
elements.sent-2411-257: Both evaluators lack the necessary granularity to differentiate malicious intent from benign expressions within complex contexts.
elements.sent-2411-258: Figures \ref{fig:eval_KM -\ref{fig:SR_2 illustrate examples of these failure cases in detail, highlighting the prevalence and types of misclassification errors in each method.}}
elements.sent-2411-259: These high misclassification rates underscore the need for a more nuanced evaluation mechanism, one capable of rigorously assessing the likelihood of truly harmful responses while reducing both over- and under-detection errors.

elements.sent-2411-286: In this section, we analyze the performance of three acquisition functions, Expected Improvement (EI), Lower Confidence Bound (LCB), and Probability of Improvement (PI), in selecting adversarial suffixes that maximize harmful response rates while adhering to query efficiency constraints.
elements.sent-2411-287: Each acquisition function exhibits unique characteristics in balancing exploration (sampling uncertain regions) and exploitation (selecting high-performing regions).
elements.sent-2411-288: Mathematically, EI is defined as: \begin{equation}\mathrm{EI}(x) = \mathbb{E}\big[\max\{ f_{\mathrm{best}} - f(x), 0\}\big],\end{equation} where the expectation is taken over the predictive distribution of \( f(x) \).
elements.sent-2411-289: EI prioritizes exploration by sampling regions with high uncertainty while favoring those likely to yield an improvement over \( f_{\text{best \).}}
elements.sent-2411-290: Its sensitivity to uncertainty in \( f(x) \) makes it effective in high-variance regions of the search space, particularly when the evaluation metric \( f(x) \) exhibits non-smooth behavior.
elements.sent-2411-291: Lower values of \( \kappa \) favor exploitation by focusing on regions with high \( \mu(x) \), while higher values encourage exploration by sampling in high-uncertainty regions.
elements.sent-2411-292: LCB is particularly useful for controlling the trade-off between minimizing harmful responses and exploring diverse adversarial suffixes.
elements.sent-2411-293: The calculation of PI is straightforward and computationally inexpensive, making it appealing for settings where quick decisions are necessary.
elements.sent-2411-294: However, its sensitivity to the predictive mean \( \mu(x) \) often results in underexploration of high-uncertainty regions, leading to premature convergence in complex search spaces.
elements.sent-2411-295: By effectively sampling regions with high uncertainty and potential improvements, EI rapidly identifies optimal adversarial suffixes, leading to a notable increase in harmful response rates, particularly after the third iteration.
elements.sent-2411-296: LCB ($\kappa = 1.96$) also performs well, albeit with a slower convergence due to its cautious trade-off between exploring uncertain regions and exploiting high-mean areas.
elements.sent-2411-297: While LCB achieves competitive ASR values by the final iteration, its initial focus on diverse sampling delays convergence to the optimal regions.
elements.sent-2411-298: In contrast, PI also achieves ASR values similar to that of LCB, primarily because its focus on immediate gains leads to underexploration of high-uncertainty regions, resulting in premature convergence.
elements.sent-2411-299: These findings underscore the necessity of a robust acquisition function like EI, which balances computational efficiency with effective exploration, to maximize harmful response rates in limited iterations while maintaining the adaptability of adversarial prompt generation.

elements.sent-2411-347: LLMs have shown impressive capabilities across various natural language processing tasks, yet remain vulnerable to input prompts, known as jailbreak attacks, carefully designed to bypass safety guardrails and elicit harmful responses.
elements.sent-2411-348: Traditional methods rely on manual heuristics but suffer from limited generalizability.
elements.sent-2411-349: Despite being automatic, optimization-based attacks often produce unnatural prompts that can be easily detected by safety filters or require high computational costs due to discrete token optimization.
elements.sent-2411-350: In this paper, we introduce Generative Adversarial Suffix Prompter (\proj), a novel automated framework that can efficiently generate human-readable jailbreak prompts in a fully black-box setting.
elements.sent-2411-351: In particular, \proj leverages latent Bayesian optimization to craft adversarial suffixes by efficiently exploring continuous latent embedding spaces, gradually optimizing the suffix prompter to improve attack efficacy while balancing prompt coherence via a targeted iterative refinement procedure.
elements.sent-2411-352: Through comprehensive experiments, we show that \proj can produce natural adversarial prompts, significantly improving jailbreak success over baselines, reducing training times, and accelerating inference speed, thus making it an efficient and scalable solution for red-teaming LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-353: Large language models (LLMs) represent a groundbreaking advancement of AI with a remarkable capacity to master a diverse set of skills, revolutionizing many areas ranging from natural language processing to code and content generation~\cite{Kojima2022, Yao2023, zhao2023survey, romera2024mathematical .}
elements.sent-2411-354: However, the versatility and strong generative capabilities of LLMs are accompanied by a critical challenge: the potential to generate toxic content~\cite{bommasani2021opportunities,goldstein2023generative .}
elements.sent-2411-355: It has been repeatedly reported that LLMs can inadvertently assimilate and perpetuate harmful biases, stereotypes, and hate speech, which are often embedded in the vast corpus of text used to train these models, raising serious ethical and societal concerns~\cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2311-18580, DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2309-07601, Liu2024790 .}
elements.sent-2411-356: The proliferation of such toxic content can facilitate the spread of misinformation, exacerbate polarization, and contribute to harassment, thereby undermining the beneficial impact of LLMs~\cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2309-13788, Wen20231322, DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2402-15302 .}
elements.sent-2411-357: To reduce the likelihood of generating harmful content, various techniques have been proposed to improve the safety alignment of LLMs~\cite{ouyang2022training, wang2023aligning, achiam2023gpt .}
elements.sent-2411-358: These approaches include red teaming~\cite{ganguli2022red and fine-tuning on meticulously curated datasets designed to reinforce positive values, ethical principles, and factual accuracy~\cite{bai2022training, DBLP:conf/nips/JiLDPZB0SW023, DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2405-17374 .}}
elements.sent-2411-359: These prompts are known as jailbreak attacks~\cite{wei2023jailbrokendoesllmsafety,shayegani2023survey , which exploit model sensitivity to input perturbations to trick LLMs into generating toxic content, albeit with considerable efforts for safety alignment.}
elements.figure-2411-022: \caption{Compared with existing jailbreak methods, \proj is fully black-box and enables high human readability, fast inference, and adaptability to diverse prompts, with support for adaptive fine-tuning.} \setlength{\tabcolsep}{5pt} 0.98\textwidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{l|ccccc} Attack Method Readability Black-Box Fast Inference Adaptive to Prompt Adaptive Fine-Tuning GCG \cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2307-15043} low \xmark \xmark \xmark \xmark AutoDAN \cite{DBLP:conf/iclr/LiuXCX24} medium \xmark \xmark \xmark \xmark AdvPrompter \cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2404-16873} medium \xmark \cmark \cmark \xmark PAIR \cite{chao2024jailbreakingblackboxlarge} medium \cmark \xmark \cmark \xmark TAP \cite{mehrotra2024treeattacksjailbreakingblackbox} high \cmark \xmark \cmark \xmark ICA \cite{wei2024jailbreakguardalignedlanguage} medium \cmark \cmark \xmark \xmark \proj (ours) high \cmark \cmark \cmark \cmark \end{tabular} } \label{table:high-level comparison GASP}
elements.sent-2411-360: To systematically understand the vulnerabilities of LLMs, existing research studies have developed a range of attack strategies to craft jailbreak prompts.
elements.sent-2411-361: Heuristic methods~\cite{jailbreakchat,wei2023jailbrokendoesllmsafety,yong2023low,shen2024donowcharacterizingevaluating,jin2024guard,wei2024jailbreakguardalignedlanguage use hand-crafted prompts to bypass safety guardrails.}
elements.sent-2411-362: While highly flexible, these methods demand extensive manual effort and often lack consistency across inputs and models, limiting their applicability.
elements.sent-2411-363: In contrast, optimization-based attacks \cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2307-15043, zhao2024acceleratinggreedycoordinategradient, DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2404-16873, li2024fastergcgefficientdiscreteoptimization, liao2024amplegcglearninguniversaltransferable, Deng_2024, jones2023automaticallyauditinglargelanguage, xu2023llmfoolitselfpromptbased, chen2024agentpoisonredteamingllmagents employ automatic search techniques by leveraging model gradients or logits to uncover prompts that are likely to elicit harmful LLM responses, offering a more robust and systematic solution.}
elements.sent-2411-364: However, these optimized prompts often deviate from natural human language, making them easily detectable through perplexity-based filtering strategies.
elements.sent-2411-365: In addition, many of these methods require complex discrete optimization over a combinatorial token space to produce a single adversarial prompt, incurring substantial computational overhead.
elements.sent-2411-366: In addition, hybrid attacks~\cite{DBLP:conf/iclr/LiuXCX24, zhu2023autodaninterpretablegradientbasedadversarial, liu2024autodanturbolifelongagentstrategy, chao2024jailbreakingblackboxlarge, mehrotra2024treeattacksjailbreakingblackbox have been developed that combine optimization techniques with manual jailbreak strategies in an automated fashion, but they often require extensive computational resources and may struggle to generalize across different scenarios (see Appendix \ref{sec:related_work for detailed discussions of related works on existing LLM jailbreak attack methods).}}
elements.sent-2411-367: Specifically, we introduce \proj\footnote{The code implementation of \proj is available at \url{https://github.com/TrustMLRG/GASP .}}
elements.sent-2411-368: Below, we highlight its key distinctive features:.
elements.sent-2411-369: By leveraging latent Bayesian optimization (LBO) \cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2111-01186, DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2207-00128, DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2310-20258 , \proj efficiently navigates in the continuous embedding space to optimize adversarial suffixes, rather than relying on discrete token optimization that is both computationally intensive and limited in search space.}
elements.sent-2411-370: This readability makes the produced jailbreak prompts much harder to detect by perplexity-based filters \cite{DBLP:conf/iclr/0002HTZF24 and jailbreak defenses, supporting effective red-teaming by simulating real-world adversarial inputs.}
elements.sent-2411-371: Through iterative fine-tuning based on odds-ratio preference optimization (ORPO) \cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2403-07691 , \proj maximizes their jailbreak potential in terms of the target LLM without compromising linguistic coherence.}
elements.sent-2411-372: Such a dual feedback loop strengthens \proj's transferability against the ever-evolving LLM safeguards.
elements.sent-2411-373: By effectively probing various configurations and uncovering vulnerabilities with minimal access, \proj enables comprehensive evaluation of model vulnerabilities, significantly strengthening red-teaming efforts.
elements.sent-2411-374: We focus on generating adversarial suffixes to jailbreak LLMs under black-box settings.
elements.sent-2411-375: This section introduces key notations and concepts to help readers better understand suffix-based jailbreak attacks.
elements.sent-2411-376: Let \( f_\theta: \mathcal{X \rightarrow \mathcal{Y \) be a \sebo{TargetLLM strengthened by some safety alignment mechanisms, where \( \mathcal{X \) is the input prompt space, \( \mathcal{Y \) is for the output response space, and \( \theta \) denotes the \sebo{TargetLLM 's parameters.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-377: A suffix-based jailbreak attack~\cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2307-15043 seeks to manipulate the \sebo{TargetLLM 's behavior by crafting an adversarial suffix that, when appended to a malicious prompt capturing the adversarial intent (e.g., ``How to build a bomb?''), causes the model to produce the corresponding harmful response (e.g., ``Sure, here is how to build a bomb...'').}}
elements.sent-2411-378: Due to the effect of safety alignment, the model response on the original input prompt typically includes refusals (e.g., ``Sorry, I cannot assist with that request,'') to react to harmful content or unethical intent embedded in the query prompt.
elements.sent-2411-379: Specifically, given a malicious prompt \( x \in \mathcal{X \) and a \sebo{TargetLLM \( f_\theta \), the objective is to find an optimal suffix \( e^* \) when appended to $x$, maximizing the probability of eliciting the ground-truth harmful response \( y \in \mathcal{Y \): \begin{equation} \label{eq:adv obj} \max_{e \in \mathcal{E}} \: \ell_\theta(e; x, y), \: \text{ where } \: \ell_\theta(e; x, y) = p_{\theta}(y \mid x + e).\end{equation}.}}}
elements.formula-2411-008: \begin{equation}\label{eq:adv obj} \max_{e \in E} \: \ell_\theta(e; x, y), \:  where  \: \ell_\theta(e; x, y) = p_{\theta}(y \mid x + e).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-380: Here, \( \mathcal{E \) stands for the collection of feasible suffixes, and \( p_{\theta (y \mid x + e) \) captures the likelihood of generating harmful responses given the modified input with respect to $f_\theta$.}}
elements.sent-2411-381: While multiple desirable responses may exist corresponding to the original harmful prompt $x$, we use the single notation \( y \) for simplicity.
elements.sent-2411-382: We note that, unlike supervised learning, the ground-truth harmful response $y$ is usually unknown when evaluating the success of a suffix-based jailbreak attack method.
elements.sent-2411-383: Alternatively, typical evaluation methods for LLM jailbreaks involve examining whether the response contains any predefined refusal strings~\cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2307-15043 or leveraging an external LLM as a judge, such as StrongREJECT~\cite{souly2024strongrejectjailbreaks .}}
elements.sent-2411-384: Therefore, we aim to ensure that the optimized suffix \( e^* \) blends into the original input \( x \) and that \( x + e^* \) appears natural and coherent.
elements.sent-2411-385: From the attacker's perspective, this is important because a prompt that is too unnatural or obfuscated may be flagged by human moderators or automated detectors~\cite{DBLP:conf/iclr/0002HTZF24 .}
elements.sent-2411-386: To enforce this, we define a readability constraint based on the probability that the modified prompt falls within the natural language distribution \( p_{\mathrm{nat \): \begin{equation}\label{eq:readability} \mathcal{E}_{\mathrm{nat}} = \{e\in\mathcal{E}: R(e; x) \geq \gamma \} , \: \text{ where } \: R(e; x) = p_{\mathrm{nat}}(x + e),\end{equation} where \( p_{\mathrm{nat (x + e) \) stands for the probability that the modified prompt \( x + e \) is perceived as part of the natural language distribution, and \( \gamma>0 \) is a hyperparameter that sets the readability threshold.}}}}

elements.sent-2411-387: With both definitions of adversarial objective and human-readability constraints in place, suffix-based jailbreak attacks can be regarded as solving the following constrained optimization problem: \begin{equation} \label{eqn:1} \max_{e \in \mathcal{E}} \ p_{\theta}(y \mid x + e), \: \text{ s.t. } \: \ p_{\mathrm{nat}}(x + e) \geq \gamma.\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-388: Equation \ref{eqn:1 guides the design of our method, ensuring that the produced suffixes bypass the safeguards of \sebo{TargetLLM $f_\theta$ while remaining natural enough to evade detection by possible safety content filters.}}
elements.sent-2411-389: Nevertheless, we focus on suffix-based jailbreaks, which aligns with the existing line of research on optimization-based attacks \cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2307-15043, DBLP:conf/iclr/LiuXCX24, DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2404-16873 , primarily due to the autoregressive nature of language models and the ease of optimization.}
elements.figure-2411-023: \includegraphics[width=0.98\linewidth]{Figures/framework.pdf} \caption{Summary of the proposed \proj framework: (A) Pre-training of \sebo{SuffixLLM} on AdvSuffixes, (B) Efficient search of adversarial suffixes in a latent space using LBO guided by real-time feedback from \sebo{TargetLLM}, (C) iterative finetuning of \sebo{SuffixLLM} with ORPO using LBO-produced suffixes, and (D) the final \sebo{SuffixLLM}'s output distribution is expected to align with \sebo{TargetLLM}.} \label{fig:summary}
elements.sent-2411-390: Recall from Section \ref{sec:background , the objective is to search for a suffix $e^*$ such that when appended to a harmful query $x$, the resulting prompt $x+e^*$ maximizes the likelihood of eliciting the desirable response $y$ from a \sebo{TargetLLM $f_\theta$, while satisfying the readability constraint.}}
elements.sent-2411-391: While different optimization-based techniques have been developed to search for jailbreak suffixes, we propose to learn a specialized \sebo{SuffixLLM \( g_\phi:\mathcal{X}\rightarrow \mathcal{E \), inspired by AdvPrompter~\cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2404-16873 , such that the output $g_{\phi (x)$ can serve as a good solution to the constrained optimization problem (Equation \ref{eqn:1 ) for any harmful query $x$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-392: At a high level, learning a suffix generator has a major advantage over other alternative optimization-based attacks.
elements.sent-2411-393: Compared with methods like GCG~\cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2307-15043 and AutoDAN~\cite{DBLP:conf/iclr/LiuXCX24 , which typically require running a discrete optimization algorithm to search through a large combinatorial token space for each harmful prompt, a generative approach can be much faster in finding jailbreak prompts at inference time (see Figure \ref{fig:times for concrete evidence).}}}
elements.sent-2411-394: From the standpoint of red-teaming LLMs, accelerating the inference speed for the generation of jailbreak prompts is desirable, which can enable the developed optimization tool to be much more scalable.
elements.sent-2411-395: Nevertheless, learning a high-performing suffix generator that is generalizable to unseen harmful prompts and adaptive to different \sebo{TargetLLM s is challenging, leading to the question of how to efficiently and effectively optimize \sebo{SuffixLLM 's parameters $\phi$.}}
elements.sent-2411-396: We note that AdvPrompter~\cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2404-16873 introduces a promising optimization framework to learn the suffix generator--alternating the search of strong jailbreak suffixes and finetuning \sebo{SuffixLLM to those suffixes--similar to the pipeline of adversarial training~\cite{madry2017towards .}}}
elements.sent-2411-397: However, AdvPrompter suffers from two major limitations: (i) lack of adaptability to the \sebo{TargetLLM $f_\theta$, since it aims to generate universal jailbreak attacks, and (ii) inefficiency in sampling strong adversarial suffixes, since it follows the optimization solver of AutoDAN that still operates in the discrete token space~\cite{zhu2023autodaninterpretablegradientbasedadversarial .}}
elements.sent-2411-398: To address the above limitations, we propose \proj, a novel attack framework with a strong emphasis on linguistic coherence, search efficiency, and targeted adaptability in a fully black-box environment.
elements.sent-2411-399: Figure \ref{fig:summary illustrates the overall pipeline of \proj, where each module will be detailed in the following sections.}
elements.sent-2411-400: Specifically, \proj first pretrains \sebo{SuffixLLM $g_{\phi}$ on a collection of generic but coherent adversarial suffixes to establish a good initialization (Section \ref{sec:AdvSuffixes ).}}
elements.sent-2411-401: Then, \sebo{SuffixLLM is refined under an alternating optimization framework, efficiently searching for strong candidate adversarial suffixes using LBO with real-time feedback from the \sebo{TargetLLM (Section \ref{sec:latent bayesian optimization ) while iteratively finetuning the generator's parameters with ORPO by prioritizing LBO-found candidate suffixes (Section \ref{sec:Iterative Generator Fine-tuning with ORPO ).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-402: Algorithms \ref{alg:train and \ref{alg:infer in Appendix \ref{sec:workflow show the pseudocode, which details how GASP is trained.}}}
elements.sent-2411-403: First, we propose to initialize \sebo{SuffixLLM by pretraining the suffix generator on AdvSuffixes, a dataset involving multiple pairs of harmful prompts and generic adversarial suffixes generated by a separate LLM (Module A in Figure \ref{fig:summary ).}}
elements.sent-2411-404: Note that establishing a meaningful initialization for \sebo{SuffixLLM is critical for enabling downstream optimization to target model-specific vulnerabilities more effectively.}
elements.sent-2411-405: In particular, we construct AdvSuffixes using a two-shot prompting technique on an uncensored LLM, applied over a range of harmful instructions originally released in AdvBench~\cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2307-15043 (see Appendix~\ref{sec:advsuffixes for more details).}}
elements.sent-2411-406: For each harmful instruction, we generate a diverse subset of adversarial suffixes for the general purpose of triggering harmful responses.
elements.sent-2411-407: These suffixes may not consistently succeed across all \sebo{TargetLLM s, but they approximate a generic adversarial distribution that captures common jailbreak patterns.}
elements.sent-2411-408: To pretrain \sebo{SuffixLLM , we apply standard Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) on our constructed AdvSuffixes dataset, training the model to generate a diverse set of harmful suffixes conditioned on a given prompt.}
elements.sent-2411-409: More rigorously, this pretraining step aims to minimize the negative log-likelihood loss over prompt-to-multiple-suffix mappings in AdvSuffixes, defined as: \begin{equation}\label{eq:pretrain SFT} L_{SFT}(\phi; AdvSuffixes) = -E_{(x, E_x) \sim \mathcal{AdvSuffixes}} \sum_{e \in E_x} \log p_\phi(e \mid x),\end{equation} where $\mathcal{E _x = \{e_1, e_2, \dots, e_k\}$ denotes a set of coherent adversarial suffixes corresponding to $x$.}
elements.formula-2411-011: \begin{equation}\label{eq:pretrain SFT} L_{SFT}(\phi; AdvSuffixes) = -E_{(x, E_x) \sim \mathcal{AdvSuffixes}} \sum_{e \in E_x} \log p_\phi(e \mid x),\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-410: According to Equation \ref{eq:pretrain SFT , \sebo{SuffixLLM is pretrained to assign high likelihood to each $e \in \mathcal{E _x$ conditioned on $x$, thereby learning a prior distribution that reflects the structure of effective jailbreak suffixes.}}}
elements.sent-2411-411: Such a prior distribution captures both linguistic fluency and adversarial potential, defining a meaningful latent space that dramatically reduces the downstream search complexity.
elements.sent-2411-412: As a result, methods like LBO can refine $p_\phi(e \mid x)$ more efficiently, avoiding the inefficiencies of training from scratch.
elements.subsection-2411-019: Efficient Search for Adversarial Suffix using LBO
elements.sent-2411-413: Although pretraining on AdvSuffixes enables \sebo{SuffixLLM to produce generic adversarial suffixes, it may be too broad to generate useful suffixes for jailbreaking \sebo{TargetLLM .}}
elements.sent-2411-414: Therefore, it is necessary to further refine \sebo{SuffixLLM 's parameters to promote a better alignment with \sebo{TargetLLM .}}
elements.sent-2411-415: However, directly optimizing the discrete token sequences is computationally expensive due to the vast number of combinatorial search cases, which will be particularly prohibitive if adopted during training.
elements.sent-2411-416: Reducing the number of search steps while maintaining the discovered adversarial suffixes with a high potential to bypass the safeguard of \sebo{TargetLLM is essential for effective finetuning.}
elements.sent-2411-417: To overcome the above challenge, \proj leverages Latent Bayesian Optimization (LBO) \cite{frazier2018tutorialbayesianoptimization to efficiently uncover jailbreak suffixes by navigating in a continuous latent space with guided feedback from \sebo{TargetLLM (Module B in Figure \ref{fig:summary ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-418: LBO is a core component of our framework, which enables a structured search over a compact latent space learned by \sebo{SuffixLLM , significantly improving the effectiveness and efficiency in discovering adversarial suffixes with high bypass potential.}
elements.sent-2411-419: To implement LBO for searching adversarial suffixes, we need first to define a few key components: (i) an encoding operation that maps any generated suffix to a latent vector, (ii) a decoding function that reconstructs any latent vector back to a corresponding adversarial suffix, and (iii) an evaluator that can give a fitness score to each latent vector, indicating how promising the corresponding suffix is.
elements.sent-2411-420: We particularly use \sebo{SuffixLLM to define this latent space, as its finetuning on AdvSuffixes enables it to capture more meaningful structure that reflects both linguistic coherence and jailbreak-inducing characteristics.}
elements.sent-2411-421: After isolating the hidden states of \sebo{SuffixLLM from the final decoder layer, we aggregate the token-level embeddings to obtain $\bm{z _e$, which encodes the suffix's global semantics, syntactic coherence, and adversarial intent as captured by \sebo{SuffixLLM .}}}
elements.sent-2411-422: Specifically, let $\mathcal{E _{x = \{e_1, e_2, \ldots, e_k\}$ denote a set of the top-promising suffixes generated from the \sebo{SuffixLLM conditioned on the harmful prompt $x$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-423: Based on the encoding operations defined above, we can then obtain the corresponding set of latent vectors $\mathcal{Z _x = \{\bm{z _{e_1 , \bm{z _{e_2 , \ldots, \bm{z _{e_k}\}$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-424: To decode a suffix from any latent vector $\bm{z}$, we perform a nearest-neighbor search using Euclidean distance in the same latent space, which can be formalized as $e({\bm{z ) = \argmin_{e_i \in \mathcal{E _x \: \| \bm{z - \bm{z _{e_i \|_2$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-425: Defining the operation in the latent space is desirable, since the encoder of \sebo{SuffixLLM is already trained such that semantically and functionally similar suffixes are mapped to nearby vectors.}
elements.sent-2411-426: As a result, nearest-neighbor decoding ensures that the reconstructed suffix $e(\bm{z )$ closely approximates the intended latent point $\bm{z}$ while preserving both adversarial effectiveness and linguistic fluency.}
elements.sent-2411-427: The output of TargetLLM is scored using GASPEval, a structured evaluation framework powered by a secondary large language model.
elements.sent-2411-428: GASPEval analyzes the response across $21$ binary criteria that capture a wide range of harmful behaviors, including hate speech, illegal instructions, misinformation, and threats.
elements.sent-2411-429: Each criterion is rated on a scale from $0$ to $2$, resulting in a fine-grained total score $s(e)$ that reflects the adversarial quality of the given suffix $e$ (see Appendix \ref{sec:gaspeval for more details).}
elements.sent-2411-430: Importantly, we adopt lazy evaluation: suffixes are evaluated only when explicitly selected by LBO's optimization loop.
elements.sent-2411-431: This avoids unnecessary scoring of the entire pool and allows the optimizer to focus computational resources on promising regions of the latent space.
elements.sent-2411-432: GP models the unknown adversarial objective function $h(\bm{z ) = s(e(\bm{z ))$ as a distribution over functions, capturing the mean estimate and uncertainty at unobserved points in the latent space.}}
elements.sent-2411-433: Using this posterior distribution, LBO employs an acquisition function $\alpha(\bm{z )$ to balance exploration and exploitation when selecting the next latent vector to evaluate.}
elements.sent-2411-434: The acquisition function quantifies the expected utility of assessing a candidate latent vector $\bm{z}$, encouraging sampling in promising regions due to either a high predicted adversarial score or high uncertainty.
elements.sent-2411-435: At each iteration, the latent vector $\bm{z _{\mathrm{next }$ that maximizes the acquisition function is selected $\bm{z _{\mathrm{next = \argmax_{\bm{z \in \mathcal{Z _x \alpha(\bm{z )$.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-436: This vector is then decoded to the nearest suffix $e_{\mathrm{next \in \mathcal{E _x$ via nearest-neighbor decoding, appended to the prompt, and scored using GASPEval.}}}
elements.sent-2411-437: Finally, the new observation $(\bm{z _{\mathrm{next , h(\bm{z _{\mathrm{next ))$ is added to the GP training set, refining the surrogate model and updating the acquisition function for the next iteration (see Appendix \ref{sec:lbo_working ).}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-438: Through this iterative process, LBO progressively narrows the search to regions of the latent space that yield more effective adversarial suffixes while maintaining linguistic fluency.
elements.sent-2411-439: The optimization concludes when the acquisition function no longer identifies promising candidates.
elements.sent-2411-440: Through LBO, we can now obtain an updated set of adversarial suffixes $\mathcal{E _x$ that have been selectively evaluated using GASPEval, based on their potential as estimated by the acquisition function and GP surrogate.}

elements.sent-2411-446: Then, we train \sebo{SuffixLLM using the ORPO loss \cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2403-07691 , which combines supervised fine-tuning and relative preference optimization: \begin{equation}\label{eq;ORPO} L_{ORPO}(\phi) = E_{(x, y_+, y_-)} \: \big[ \ell_{SFT}(\phi; x, y_{+}) + \lambda \cdot \ell_{OR}(\phi; x, y_{+}, y_{-}) \big],\end{equation} where $\ell_{\mathrm{SFT }$ is the cross-entropy loss for generating the preferred suffix $y_+$, $\ell_{\mathrm{OR }$ captures the odds ratio between the likelihood of generating preferred $y_+$ and disfavored $y_-$, and $\lambda\geq 0$ controls the trade-off (Module C in Figure \ref{fig:summary ).}}}}}
