elements.algorithm-2411-003: \caption{GASPTrain: PreTraining \& FineTuning of \sebo{SuffixLLM}} \setstretch{1.1} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \REQUIRE AdvSuffixes $\mathcal{D}_{\mathrm{adv}}$, \sebo{TargetLLM} $f_\theta$, split ratio $\beta \in (0,1)$, batch size $B$, number of training epochs ($S_1$, $S_2$), learning rates ($\eta_1$, $\eta_2$) \ENSURE Finetuned \sebo{SuffixLLM} $g_\phi$ \STATE Randomly split $\mathcal{D}_{\mathrm{adv}}$ into $\mathcal{D}_{\mathrm{pre}}$ (first $\beta$ percentage) and $\mathcal{D}_{\mathrm{fine}}$ (remaining $(1-\beta)$) \STATE \textit{// Stage 1: Pretraining \sebo{SuffixLLM} using SFT} \STATE Initialize $g_{\phi}$ \FOR{$s = 1,2,\ldots, S_1$} \STATE $\mathcal{B}_{\mathrm{pre}} = \{(x_i, \mathcal{E}_{i})\}_{i=1}^{B} \leftarrow$ Sample a batch from $\mathcal{D}_{\mathrm{pre}}$ \STATE $\phi \leftarrow \phi - \eta_1 \cdot \nabla L_{\mathrm{SFT}}(\phi; \mathcal{B_{\mathrm{pre}}})$, where $L_{\mathrm{SFT}}$ follows Equation \ref{eq:pretrain SFT} \ENDFOR \STATE \textit{// Stage 2: Finetuning \sebo{SuffixLLM} with LBO using ORPO} \FOR{$s = 1,2,\ldots, S_2$} \STATE $\mathcal{B}_{\mathrm{fine}} = \{(x_i, \mathcal{E}_{i})\}_{i=1}^{B} \leftarrow$ Sample a batch from $\mathcal{D}_{\mathrm{fine}}$ \FOR{each $(x, \mathcal{E})$ in $\mathcal{B}_{\mathrm{fine}}$} \STATE $\tilde{\mathcal{E}}_{x} \leftarrow$ apply Algorithm \ref{alg:lbo} on $x$ with $g_{\phi}$ to obtain a sorted list of suffixes (by scores) \ENDFOR \STATE $\tilde{\mathcal{B}}_{\mathrm{fine}} = \{(x_i, \tilde{\mathcal{E}}_{x_i})\}_{i=1}^{B}$ \STATE $\phi \leftarrow \phi - \eta_2 \cdot \nabla L_{\mathrm{ORPO}}(\phi; \tilde{\mathcal{B}}_{\mathrm{fine}})$, where $L_{\mathrm{ORPO}}$ follows Equation \ref{eq;ORPO} \ENDFOR \end{algorithmic} \label{alg:train}
elements.algorithm-2411-004: \caption{GASPInfer: Inference-Time Generation of Adversarial Suffix} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \setstretch{1.1} \REQUIRE Test prompt $x$, final \sebo{SuffixLLM} $g_{\phi}$, evaluator $\mathrm{GASPEval}(\cdot)$, \sebo{TargetLLM} $f_\theta$ \ENSURE Adversarial suffix $\hat{e}$ \STATE $\tilde{\mathcal{E}}_{x} \leftarrow$ apply Algorithm \ref{alg:lbo} on $x$ with $g_{\phi}$ to obtain a list of suffixes generated by $g_{\phi}$ \STATE \textcolor{gray}{// Since $\tilde{\mathcal{E}}_x$ is sorted by GASPEval score (ascending), no need to reevaluate} \STATE Output the most effective suffix $\hat{e} = \tilde{\mathcal{E}}_x[0]$ \end{algorithmic} \label{alg:infer}
elements.algorithm-2411-005: \caption{LBO Search for Efficient Suffix Generalization} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \setstretch{1.15} \REQUIRE Prompt $x$, (intermediate or final) \sebo{SuffixLLM} $g_{\phi}$, evaluator $\mathrm{GASPEval}(\cdot)$, \sebo{TargetLLM} $f_\theta$, number of generated suffixes $k$, number of initial vectors $u$, query budget parameter $M$ \ENSURE A list of sorted suffixes $\tilde{\mathcal{E}}_x$ (with sorted scores $h(z_e)$ corresponding to $\mathcal{E}_{\mathcal{A}}$) \STATE $\mathcal{E}_x = \{e_1, \ldots, e_k\} \leftarrow$ Generate $k$ suffixes by prompting $g_{\phi}$ \FOR{$i = 1, 2, \ldots, k$} \STATE $z_{e_i} = \mathrm{Enc_{\phi}}(x + e_i) \leftarrow$ Compute final-layer latent representation \ENDFOR \STATE $\mathcal{Z}_x \gets \{z_{e_1}, \ldots, z_{e_k}\}$, and $\mathcal{Z}_{\mathrm{init}} \gets$ randomly sample a subset of size $u$ from $\mathcal{Z}_{x}$ \STATE $\mathcal{A} = \{(z_e, h(z_e)): z_e\in\mathcal{Z}_{\mathrm{init}}, h(z_e) = \mathrm{GASPEval}(x + e, f_\theta(x + e))\}$ \WHILE{$|\mathcal{A}| \leq M$ and $\min_{(z, h(z)) \in \mathcal{A}} h(z) > 0$} \STATE $\mathcal{GP}(\mu_{\mathcal{A}}(z), \sigma_{\mathcal{A}}(z)) \gets$ Fit a GP surrogate model based on the observed pairs in $\mathcal{A}$ \STATE $\alpha(z) \gets $ Define an acquisition function based on $\mathcal{GP}(\mu_{\mathcal{A}}(z), \sigma_{\mathcal{A}}(z))$ \STATE $z_{\mathrm{next}} = \argmin_{z \in \mathcal{Z}_x \setminus \mathcal{A}_1} \alpha(z)$, where $\mathcal{A}_1 = \{z:(z,h(z))\in\mathcal{A}\}$ \STATE $e_{\mathrm{next}} = \argmin_{e_i \in \mathcal{E}_x} \| z_{\mathrm{next}} - z_{e_i} \|_2 \gets$ decode a suffix from $z_{\mathrm{next}}$ \STATE $h(z_{\mathrm{next}}) = \mathrm{GASPEval}(x+e_{\text{next}}, f_\theta(x+e_{\text{next}}))$ \STATE $\mathcal{A} \gets \mathcal{A} \cup \{(z_{\text{next}}, h(z_{\text{next}}))\}$ \ENDWHILE \STATE Sort $\mathcal{A}$ and the corresponding suffixes $\mathcal{E}_{\mathcal{A}}$ in ascending order of $h(z_{e})$ \STATE $\tilde{\mathcal{E}}_x \gets$ Append all unevaluated suffixes $\mathcal{E}_x \setminus \mathcal{E}_{\mathcal{A}}$ to $\mathcal{E}_{\mathcal{A}}$ \end{algorithmic} \label{alg:lbo}

(b) Plot of Loss and NLL Loss during ORPO training for the target black-box model LLama-3.1-8B, showing changes over iterations.}
elements.sent-2411-548: For adversarial prompting, a central challenge is the inability to directly access the target model's probability distribution \( p_{\theta (y \mid x + e) \), where \( y \) represents the desired harmful responses given a prompt \( x \) and an appended suffix \( e \).}
elements.sent-2411-549: Given a list of $k$ suffixes generated by \sebo{SuffixLLM , the core task of LBO is to identify suffixes that are most likely to produce harmful outputs when appended to a harmful instruction.}
elements.sent-2411-550: Since querying the \sebo{TargetLLM is expensive, LBO aims to minimize the number of evaluations by strategically exploring the suffix space using a surrogate Gaussian Process (GP) model and an acquisition function.}
elements.sent-2411-551: We empirically justify this design choice in Appendix \ref{sec:lbo_layer .}
elements.sent-2411-552: This results in a fixed set of latent vectors $\mathcal{Z _x = \{z_{e_1 , z_{e_2 , \dots, z_{e_k}\} \subset \mathbb{R ^d$ corresponding to the suffix candidates.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-553: Because obtaining the score $h(z)$ is expensive, we treat it as a black-box function and approximate it using a GP, a probabilistic surrogate model that defines a distribution over possible functions: \begin{equation}h(z) \sim GP(\mu(z), k(z, z')).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-554: Here, $\mu(z)$ is the prior mean function (typically assumed to be zero), and $k(z, z')$ is the covariance kernel function that encodes assumptions about the smoothness and similarity between function values at different points in the latent space.
elements.sent-2411-555: To be more specific, given a set of $t$ observed suffixes and their scores $\mathcal{A = \{z_{e_j , h(z_{e_j )\}_{j=1 ^t$, the GP posterior yields, for any new latent vector $z \in \mathcal{Z _x$, a predictive distribution over the possible values of $h(z)$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-556: This predictive distribution is Gaussian: \begin{equation}h(z) \big|_{A} \sim N(\mu_{A}(z), \sigma_{A}^2(z)).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-557: This means that, conditioned on the observed data, the model believes that the unknown score at $z$ is normally distributed with mean $\mu_{\mathcal{A (z)$, the GP's best estimate of $h(z)$, and variance $\sigma_{\mathcal{A ^2(z)$ representing its uncertainty about this estimate.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-558: These quantities are derived analytically based on the kernel and the observed dataset $\mathcal{A}$.
elements.sent-2411-559: Such a GP modeling step thus provides a principled mechanism to quantify both the predicted score at each point and the model's confidence in that prediction, which are critical for guiding the next evaluation point via an acquisition function.
elements.sent-2411-560: Since we seek to minimize $h(z)$ (lower scores indicate more effective jailbreaks), a common choice is Lower Confidence Bound (LCU): $\alpha(z) = \mu_\mathcal{A (z) - \beta \cdot \sigma_\mathcal{A (z)$, where $\beta > 0$ is a tunable parameter controlling exploration.}}
elements.sent-2411-561: In our experiments, we tested three common heuristic choices of acquisition function for Bayesian optimization, including Probability of Improvement, Lower Confidence Bound, and Expected Improvement (see Figure \ref{fig:acq_study and Section \ref{sec:acquisition function sensitivity for our ablations on acquisition function).}}
elements.sent-2411-562: Based on the selected acquisition function, the next suffix to query is chosen as: \begin{equation}z_{next} = \argmin_{z \in Z_x \setminus A_1} \alpha(z),  where  A_1 = \{z:(z,h(z))\inA\}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-563: Note that to avoid repeated evaluations, we exclude all the observed suffixes (and their latent vectors).
elements.sent-2411-564: Our GP model guides the acquisition function by continuously updating the posterior mean and uncertainty as more suffixes are evaluated.
elements.sent-2411-565: The acquisition function, in turn, exploits this posterior to identify the most promising unseen latent vector.
elements.sent-2411-566: This feedback loop narrows down the search in the latent space $\mathcal{Z _x$, prioritizing areas with high probability of low $h(z)$ (i.e., high harmfulness).}
elements.sent-2411-567: This latent vector is decoded and a final suffix is chosen, as described in Section \ref{sec:latent bayesian optimization and Algorithm \ref{alg:lbo .}}
elements.sent-2411-568: We further provide a sensitivity study on the query budget defined in Algorithm \ref{alg:lbo in Appendix \ref{sec:query_budget .}}
elements.sent-2411-569: Rather than evaluating all generated suffixes with the expensive \sebo{TargetLLM , LBO drastically reduces query cost.}
elements.sent-2411-570: By fitting a GP over a small subset of latent suffix representations, LBO intelligently selects only the most promising candidates to evaluate with the expensive GASPEval function.
elements.sent-2411-571: This avoids the combinatorial explosion of scoring all $k$ suffixes and instead converges to high-quality adversarial suffixes in far fewer evaluations (typically $u + O(\log M) \ll k$).
elements.sent-2411-572: GASPEval is a dual-purpose component within our framework.
elements.sent-2411-573: It is designed to steer the LBO process through the complex probability landscape and serve as a comprehensive evaluation mechanism for adversarial prompts generated by different jailbreak attack methods.
elements.figure-2411-030: \includegraphics[width=0.65\linewidth]{Figures/lbo.pdf} \caption{Illustration of navigation in the distribution landscape with LBO. Data points, scored by GASPEval, reveal a path through regions of high probability.} \label{fig:lbo_algo}
elements.sent-2411-574: On the one hand, GASPEval guides LBO by providing structured feedback during optimization, allowing the search to efficiently navigate the continuous latent space of suffix embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-575: By scoring each decoded suffix, GASPEval enables the GP surrogate model to refine its posterior over the score function, thereby narrowing down high-potential regions of the space and improving adversarial success rates (see Figure \ref{fig:lbo_algo for an illustration of this process).}
elements.sent-2411-576: On the other hand, GASPEval acts as a robust evaluator, balancing the shortcomings of prior metrics.
elements.sent-2411-577: While StrongREJECT tends to over-penalize borderline prompts, reducing exploration, and keyword-based heuristics suffer from high false positive/negative rates, GASPEval offers a moderate, consistent scoring mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-578: This dual role, guidance for optimization and reliable evaluation, makes it central to the success of both LBO and the broader \proj pipeline.
elements.sent-2411-579: In addition, StrongREJECT, an aggressive filtering method aimed at blocking adversarial inputs, frequently misses subtle yet harmful responses, resulting in FNs that allow genuinely harmful content to evade detection.

elements.sent-2411-601: In this section, we study the effect of acquisition functions (EI, LCB, and PI) on selecting suffixes that maximize harmful response rates while maintaining query efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-602: Different acquisition functions balance exploration (sampling uncertain regions) and exploitation (selecting high-performing regions) differently.
elements.sent-2411-603: For instance, Expected Improvement (EI) prioritizes suffixes likely to improve upon previous results while sampling in uncertain regions, making it particularly effective in high-variance search spaces.
elements.sent-2411-604: Lower Confidence Bound (LCB) controls the trade-off between exploration and exploitation through a tunable parameter, allowing a more cautious search strategy.
elements.sent-2411-605: Probability of Improvement (PI) focuses on selecting suffixes with high immediate gains but tends to under-explore uncertain regions, which can lead to premature convergence.
elements.sent-2411-606: Our comparative analysis in Figure~\ref{fig:acq_study shows that EI consistently outperforms the other strategies, achieving the highest attack success rate (ASR) within a few iterations.}
elements.sent-2411-607: LCB follows closely but converges more slowly due to its conservative sampling approach.
elements.sent-2411-608: PI, while computationally efficient, tends to stagnate early.
elements.sent-2411-609: These trends hold across different models, including Mistral-7B-v0.3 and Falcon-7B, reinforcing the effectiveness of EI in adversarial prompt generation.

elements.sent-2411-659: Specifically, given a malicious prompt \( x \in \mathcal{X \) and a \sebo{TargetLLM \( f_\theta \), the objective is to find an optimal suffix \( e^* \) when appended to $x$, maximizing the probability of eliciting the ground-truth harmful response \( y \in \mathcal{Y \): \begin{equation}\label{eq:adv obj} \max_{e \in \mathcal{E}} \: \ell_\theta(e; x, y), \: \text{ where } \: \ell_\theta(e; x, y) = p_{\theta}(y \mid x + e).\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-660: With both definitions of adversarial objective and human-readability constraints in place, suffix-based jailbreak attacks can be regarded as solving the following constrained optimization problem: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:1} \max_{e \in \mathcal{E}} \ p_{\theta}(y \mid x + e), \: \text{ s.t. } \: \ p_{\mathrm{nat}}(x + e) \geq \gamma.\end{equation}.

elements.subsection-2411-035: On Query Cost as a Fair Comparison Metric
elements.sent-2411-828: Existing jailbreak attack papers often differ widely in how they measure or report efficiency, making cross-method comparisons challenging.
elements.sent-2411-829: In particular, methods like AmpleGCG \cite{liao2024amplegcglearninguniversaltransferable involve multi-phase pipelines, first overgenerating and filtering successful suffixes using large white-box models, then training separate generators, whereas GASP operates fully online with a fixed and transparent query budget to the \sebo{TargetLLM .}}
elements.sent-2411-830: For example, AmpleGCG achieves an ASR of $84\%$ after $74$K queries, while attacking the white-box model Llama-3-8B.
elements.sent-2411-831: However, GASP achieves an ASR of $68\%$ with a much lesser query budget of $2.5$K queries ($29.6\times$ lesser).
elements.sent-2411-832: We argue that query cost to the target model should serve as the primary metric for fair comparison among black-box jailbreak attacks.
elements.sent-2411-833: This metric reflects real-world constraints, where model access is limited and monetized.
elements.sent-2411-834: Unlike indirect proxies such as training time or number of generated suffixes, query cost captures the actual resource expenditure and attack feasibility.
elements.sent-2411-835: Future research should adopt standardized query-normalized benchmarks, reporting success rates as a function of the number of queries, to enable principled evaluation of efficiency-effectiveness trade-offs across methods.
